input.prompt:
My MSTest project has a .testsettings file that configures test deployment, timeout,
and a code coverage data collector. After upgrading MSTest packages, the settings
seem to be ignored. How do I migrate this configuration?

data.reasoningText: The user has an MSTest project with a `.testsettings` file and wants to migrate to the newer `.runsettings` format. Let me look at their project files to understand the current configuration.

arguments.file_text:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<RunSettings>
  <RunConfiguration>
    <!-- 30-second per-test timeout (was TestTimeout in .testsettings Properties) -->
    <TestSessionTimeout>30000</TestSessionTimeout>
  </RunConfiguration>

  <MSTest>
    <!-- Per-test method timeout matching the original 30 s value -->
    <TestTimeout>30000</TestTimeout>
    <!-- Assembly resolution / deployment is handled automatically;
         set DeploymentEnabled=false if you never need file deployment -->
  </MSTest>

  <DataCollectionRunSettings>
    <DataCollectors>
      <DataCollector friendlyName="Code Coverage"
                     uri="datacollector://Microsoft/CodeCoverage/2.0"
                     assemblyQualifiedName="Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector.CoverageCollectorDataCollector, Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector, Culture=neutral">
        <Configuration>
          <CodeCoverage>
            <!-- Customize include/exclude filters here if needed -->
          </CodeCoverage>
        </Configuration>
      </DataCollector>
    </DataCollectors>
  </DataCollectionRunSettings>
</RunSettings>

data.reasoningText:
The user has a `.testsettings` file which is the legacy format, and they've upgraded to MSTest 3.8.0. The `.testsettings` format is no longer supported in modern MSTest/vstest. They need to migrate to a `.runsettings` file.

Let me create the equivalent `.runsettings` file and update the `.csproj` to reference it.

The `.testsettings` file has:
1. Assembly resolution with `useLoadContext="true"` - this is default behavior in modern MSTest
2. TestTimeout of 30000ms (30 seconds)
3. Code Coverage data collector


4. Now I'm translating these settings into the `.runsettings` format, which uses a different XML structure with RunConfiguration and DataCollector sections to preserve the timeout and coverage settings.

arguments.file_text:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<RunSettings>
  <RunConfiguration>
    <!-- 30-second per-test timeout (was TestTimeout in .testsettings Properties) -->
    <TestSessionTimeout>30000</TestSessionTimeout>
  </RunConfiguration>

  <MSTest>
    <!-- Per-test method timeout matching the original 30 s value -->
    <TestTimeout>30000</TestTimeout>
    <!-- Assembly resolution / deployment is handled automatically;
         set DeploymentEnabled=false if you never need file deployment -->
  </MSTest>

  <DataCollectionRunSettings>
    <DataCollectors>
      <DataCollector friendlyName="Code Coverage"
                     uri="datacollector://Microsoft/CodeCoverage/2.0"
                     assemblyQualifiedName="Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector.CoverageCollectorDataCollector, Microsoft.VisualStudio.TraceCollector, Culture=neutral">
        <Configuration>
          <CodeCoverage>
            <!-- Customize include/exclude filters here if needed -->
          </CodeCoverage>
        </Configuration>
      </DataCollector>
    </DataCollectors>
  </DataCollectionRunSettings>
</RunSettings>

data.content: Now wire the `.runsettings` file into the project:

Here's the migration summary:

**`.testsettings` → `.runsettings`** — MSTest v3 ignores the legacy `.testsettings` format. I created `local.runsettings` with the equivalent settings:

| `.testsettings` setting | `.runsettings` equivalent |
|---|---|
| `<Property name="TestTimeout" value="30000" />` | `<MSTest><TestTimeout>30000</TestTimeout>` + `<TestSessionTimeout>` |
| Code Coverage `DataCollector` (uri v1.0) | `DataCollector` with updated v2.0 URI |
| `AssemblyResolution / useLoadContext` | No longer needed — handled automatically |

The `.csproj` now points to the new file via `<RunSettingsFilePath>`. You can safely delete `local.testsettings` once you've confirmed tests run correctly with `dotnet test`.
